# Questions in Livestream 244

Live Stream #244: Romani Ite Domum

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=498" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:18</a> | Is red and orange a Catholic color? | He says maybe, perhaps in Ireland, but he is not sure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:32</a> | If there is no external evidence for Matthew's star of Bethlehem, should it be taken as allegorical? | No; he says it should be read in ancient astrological terms, not as an allegory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=787" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:07</a> | Is astrology not entirely fake? | He says it is not a legitimate science, but the passage records what ancient astrologers believed rather than endorsing astrology as true. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:58</a> | Can it be said that astrology and astronomy were conflated then? | He says there was not astronomy in the modern sense then, so astrology filled the role of interpreting the sky. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=933" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:33</a> | Did elephants, rhinos, and cheetahs once roam the ancient Near East? | He treats elephants and cheetahs as plausible in the wider region, but says rhinos were not really part of the historical Near Eastern picture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=1033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:13</a> | Does he support the shorter 10-11 year reign or longer 14-15 year reign for Seti I? | He supports the 14-15 year reign and rejects the idea that Ramesses II could simply absorb years from a co-regency into his own reign. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=1257" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:57</a> | How should one respond to the early-date argument that Exodus 14:2 toponyms could have existed earlier even if texts preserving them have not survived? | He says that might work for some Exodus 14:2 names, but it fails for broader Exodus toponyms such as Migdol, Pithom, and Ramesses, whose historical settings are later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=1670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:50</a> | Why did Samson and the prince of Shechem depend on their fathers to arrange their marriages? | First marriages were normally arranged by parents in the ancient Near East; arranging a good match was treated as responsible parenting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=1803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:03</a> | If an ancient Near Eastern person was still unmarried at 45, would a first marriage still be arranged by the parents? | If the parents were still alive, probably yes; he says the parents would likely be unhappy that the person was still unmarried. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=1925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:05</a> | Would anyone have thought about eloping back then? | It was done, but with intense shame attached and potentially severe consequences from the woman's family. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=2101" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:01</a> | Was Ezekiel 29:17-21 unfulfilled, and could Persia have taken Babylon's role? | He says Nebuchadnezzar did take Ty, and the prophecy involves Babylon and Egypt rather than Persia replacing Babylon's role. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=2625" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:45</a> | What likely happened to Sennacherib's army in Isaiah 37:36? | He thinks a fast-moving camp disease, specifically cholera from contaminated water and poor sanitation, is the best explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=3024" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:24</a> | In 1 Chronicles 7:21, were Ephraim's sons stealing livestock in Gath while they should have been in Egypt? | No. He reads the passage as later clan history after the conquest and says the Gathites, not Ephraim's descendants, were probably the livestock thieves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=3396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:36</a> | Was there a charge for being drunk and disorderly in the ancient Near East? | Usually no by itself; it became punishable when drunkenness led to other crimes or serious public disorder. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=3542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:02</a> | What are the biggest problems with the Levite hypothesis? | He says it depends heavily on the documentary hypothesis, assumes an implausibly tiny exodus and quiet infiltration, and does not fit destruction evidence such as Jericho and Hazor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=3903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:03</a> | If the exodus group was only about 2,000 people, would Pharaoh even have cared? | He says no, and also argues that such a small group would not realistically be enough to conquer places like Jericho on its own. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:41</a> | Are cursed objects such as dolls or books a real thing? | He treats the subject cautiously, saying the evidence is too thin to make sweeping claims and that the term "cursed object" covers several different ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:04</a> | If a fortune teller uses stylized cards, do those gain the taint of evil? | They could, but not necessarily; he says psychological, pharmacological, and spiritual factors may all be involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4416" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:36</a> | What about holy objects? | He says holy objects are consistently used for holy purposes and that sacred spaces such as churches can carry a sense of peace and sanctity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4504" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:04</a> | Was an object placed outside a church cursed, or were spirits hanging around? | He says probably spirits hanging around, because a curse is an incantation rather than a residual effect inside an object. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4679" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:59</a> | If you found a newly opened tomb with an ancient curse on it, would you open it? | Yes; he says the so-called mummy's curse is fake folklore rather than something archaeologists should treat as real. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4801" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:01</a> | Did Egyptian tomb robbers ignore tomb curses, or just fail to read them? | He says most Egyptian tomb curses were not really anti-entry traps at all, but ritual-purity warnings in tombs that were often meant to receive public offerings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=4911" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:51</a> | How is possession different from mental illness? | He says symptoms can overlap, so mental illness should be assessed first; the clearest distinction would be preternatural abilities such as unknown languages or levitation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=5133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:33</a> | What criteria are used to differentiate alleged demonic possession from mental or neurological disorder? | He treats it as more pastoral than scholarly and again points to preternatural abilities as the main marker. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=5334" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:54</a> | Are there modern cases of levitation or telekinesis? | He says yes, claiming there have been several well-documented cases of both. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=5378" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:38</a> | Do modern governments contact spiritual beings? | Not routinely; he distinguishes possession from influence and says governments may be spiritually influenced through ordinary moral corruption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=5598" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:18</a> | Does God possess and experience emotions? | Yes, but not as changeable human emotions; he describes God's emotional response in terms of proximity to holiness, sin, and his people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=5790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:30</a> | How accurate were Nubian bowmen in battles with the Egyptians? | He says they were deadly accurate and notes that Nubia was known as the land of the bowmen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=5872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:52</a> | Did the loss of Egyptian chariots in the exodus contribute to the later weakening of dynasties 19 and 20? | Yes; he argues chariot corps take decades to rebuild because they require horses, wood imports, and long training pipelines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=6338" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:38</a> | Did the Israelites choose bovine worship at Mount Sinai because bovines were prominent Egyptian deities? | He says bovine worship was ubiquitous in Egypt, but argues the specific fit is the son of Hesat myth, where a dead deity is reborn as a golden male calf. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=6534" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:54</a> | Did Jeroboam get the idea for his cult images from the Sinai golden calf? | No; he says Jeroboam's bull imagery fits Baal worship rather than a reuse of the Sinai calf episode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=6613" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:13</a> | Were surrounding Canaanite cultures influenced by Egypt because of Egypt's earlier power over the region? | Egypt had influence, especially artistic and iconographic, but he says religious influence was more limited and often ran from Canaan into Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=7155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:15</a> | Why is the benediction for the blessed dead, ma'a-kheru, used for Ra and Amenhotep IV's prenomen? | He says ma'a-kheru means true of voice or speaking truly, so in that setting it does not mean Ra is dead but that Ra speaks truly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=7499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:59</a> | How can one determine whether the early chapters of Genesis are factual history, hyperbole, metaphor, or something else? | He says hermeneutics there is hard and requires attention to ancient Near Eastern ritual, sacred space, and the foundation those chapters provide for tabernacle theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=7837" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:37</a> | During Ramesses II's reign and the exodus, was the Egyptian capital at Qantir or at Thebes? | He says the capital was at Qantir, with the move happening early in Ramesses II's reign as part of shifting the royal power base. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=7972" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:52</a> | If Ramesses II had many children, why would losing one firstborn son be significant enough to release the Israelites? | Because the firstborn was Amun-her-khepeshef, a powerful adult crown prince and key administrator, not merely one replaceable child among many. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=8306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:26</a> | Was Amun-her-khepeshef the one who died during Moses's time? | He thinks so, placing the death around Ramesses II's 25th regnal year, the period where he expects the exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=8464" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:04</a> | Could Ramesses II's arthritis explain why he was not personally at the Reed Sea incident? | Possibly; he says arthritis is one reasonable explanation, especially since an older king would not necessarily remain an active chariot campaigner. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=8573" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:53</a> | How can Christians best apply Joshua's concept in conquest to life? | He gives only a brief direct answer, saying Joshua acted on faith and trusted in the Lord, before moving to related discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=8942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:02</a> | Is it best to argue that high Christology already appears in the New Testament and Nicaea formulated rather than originated it? | Yes; he says the New Testament and early church fathers already support high Christology, while Nicaea formulated it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9114" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:54</a> | Is the award for Requiem of the Dead an award for the book or for the author? | It is an award for the book because the book was judged, though he notes it is also his award as the author. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:44</a> | Is it acceptable to use money normally tithed to donate to a local food bank? | If someone is associated with a church, he says the tithe should go to the local church and giving beyond that can support a food bank or other ministry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:09</a> | Who judges the authors in a literary contest? | He says literary contests judge books through panels that may include other authors, media experts, publishers, editors, former winners, or similar reviewers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:24</a> | Was the similar wording in Exodus 4:19 and Matthew 2:20 intentional or coincidental? | He thinks it was probably intentional, noting the similarity between the two divine-revelation scenes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:02</a> | What is his opinion of Zach from What Your Pastor Didn't Tell You? | He gives a sharply negative personal assessment and says Zach is not welcome on the channel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9637" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:37</a> | Can he elaborate on the archaeology from Ai? | He says the identification remains uncertain, rejects Tel Maqatir as a good candidate, and says et-Tell remains possible if the relevant destruction or site-leveling evidence holds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=9877" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:37</a> | Can he clarify Ecclesiastes 10:11 in relation to a claim that the Quran's Satan-whispering language comes from reading nahash as lahash? | He says nahash and lahash are not the same word, though Ecclesiastes 10:11 can involve wordplay around serpent and whispering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=10188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:48</a> | Do the words for snake and whisper sound similar enough to create wordplay? | He says they can create wordplay, but no ancient Semitic speaker would confuse them as the same word. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=10474" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:34</a> | Did Shoshenq reference the house of David? | He is not sure without looking it up, but says Shoshenq does refer to the house of the king and to several Judean towns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=10553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:53</a> | Does he know the article about Late Bronze Age and Iron Age artifacts at et-Tell? | He says the relevant source is Joseph Callaway's report on et-Tell, which dates material around 1200 BC rather than using his preferred period labels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=10686" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:06</a> | Which eschatology is he leaning toward: pre, post, amillennialism, premillennialism, literal, figurative, and how much preterism? | He identifies his view as post-tribulation historic premillennial, rejects dispensational premillennialism, and says he does not like preterism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=10790" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:50</a> | Does the kneeling figure associated with Jehu on the Black Obelisk represent Jehu himself or an Israelite servant tribute bearer? | He thinks it is Jehu himself because the Assyrian caption identifies him and vassal kings were expected to appear and present tribute. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=11407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:07</a> | Would he consider writing, editing, or contributing to a work about the historicity of the exodus? | He has already contributed a chapter elsewhere and is working on another possible project, but says publisher approval remains uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=11971" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:31</a> | How should one understand the monk Hyperechius's statement about the serpent driving Eve from paradise by whispering to her? | He says that language makes him think of Ophite or Gnostic influence, with the serpent whispering secret knowledge to Eve. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=12145" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:25</a> | Did he see Craig Evans promoting the Mount Ebal curse amulet? | Yes; he says Evans should stay out of Old Testament archaeology and that the amulet claim had already been discredited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=12268" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:28</a> | How is the Mount Ebal curse amulet discredited? | He says there is no readable inscription on it beyond perhaps a possible H, and that the rest is pareidolia. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=12629" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:29</a> | What was the highest carbon-14 date for material from Mount Ebal? | He says the carbon-14 and ceramic evidence point around 1200 to 1150 BC, in the Late Bronze 2B to Iron Age 1 range. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IK4BGi8PxBU?t=13068" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:48</a> | If the Mount Ebal object has no writing on it, why call it an amulet? | He says he would call it a weight, but the name amulet stuck because ABR and Stripling used it. |
